While the security boundary for protecting API resources lies in FastAPI, it is often desirable to protect certain routes or components in the SPA such that unauthenticated users are redirected to login before accessing them. The Auth0 React SDK provides the `withAuthenticationRequired` higher-order component that wraps a React component and ensures that visiting it triggers login when the user is not authenticated.[26][29] For example, you can create a protected route component as follows:

```javascript
// src/components/ProtectedRoute.js
import React from "react";
import { withAuthenticationRequired } from "@auth0/auth0-react";

function ProtectedContent() {
  return <div>Only authenticated users can see this.</div>;
}

export default withAuthenticationRequired(ProtectedContent, {
  onRedirecting: () => <div>Redirecting to login…</div>,
});
```

When integrated into your route configuration, navigation to this component will automatically redirect the user to Auth0’s Universal Login if they are not already authenticated.[26][29] This mechanism ensures that access to private React routes correlates with Auth0 session state, although the backend still enforces access to underlying data via token validation.  

### Calling the FastAPI API with Access Tokens

The central intersection between the React SPA and the FastAPI API occurs when the SPA needs to call backend endpoints that require authorization. Auth0’s React documentation explains that the SPA must obtain an access token from Auth0 in a format the API can verify by passing the audience of the API when requesting tokens, and then pass that token as a Bearer credential in the Authorization header of the HTTP request.[6][6][26][29] The Auth0 React SDK’s `getAccessTokenSilently` method handles token acquisition and renewal without prompting the user for credentials again, as long as the Auth0 session remains valid.[6][6][29]  

A simple API client component might look like this:

```javascript
// src/components/ApiClient.js
import React, { useState } from "react";
import { useAuth0 } from "@auth0/auth0-react";

const apiServerUrl = process.env.REACT_APP_API_SERVER_URL;

export function ApiClient() {
  const { isAuthenticated, getAccessTokenSilently } = useAuth0();
  const [publicMessage, setPublicMessage] = useState("");
  const [privateMessage, setPrivateMessage] = useState("");
  const [rbacMessage, setRbacMessage] = useState("");
  const [error, setError] = useState("");

  async function callPublic() {
    setError("");
    const response = await fetch(`${apiServerUrl}/api/public`);
    const data = await response.json();
    setPublicMessage(data.message);
  }

  async function callPrivate() {
    setError("");
    try {
      const token = await getAccessTokenSilently();
      const response = await fetch(`${apiServerUrl}/api/private`, {
        headers: {
          Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`,
        },
      });
      if (!response.ok) {
        const body = await response.json();
        throw new Error(body.detail || "Request failed");
      }
      const data = await response.json();
      setPrivateMessage(data.message);
    } catch (e) {
      setError(e.message);
    }
  }

  async function callRbacEndpoint() {
    setError("");
    try {
      const token = await getAccessTokenSilently();
      const response = await fetch(`${apiServerUrl}/api/messages`, {
        headers: {
          Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`,
        },
      });
      if (!response.ok) {
        const body = await response.json();
        throw new Error(body.detail || "Request failed");
      }
      const data = await response.json();
      setRbacMessage(data.message);
    } catch (e) {
      setError(e.message);
    }
  }

  return (
    <div>
      <button onClick={callPublic}>Call Public API</button>
      {publicMessage && <p>Public: {publicMessage}</p>}

      {isAuthenticated && (
        <>
          <button onClick={callPrivate}>Call Private API</button>
          {privateMessage && <p>Private: {privateMessage}</p>}

          <button onClick={callRbacEndpoint}>Call RBAC API</button>
          {rbacMessage && <p>RBAC: {rbacMessage}</p>}
        </>
      )}

      {error && <p style={{ color: "red" }}>Error: {error}</p>}
    </div>
  );
}
```

When `getAccessTokenSilently` is called, the Auth0 React SDK requests a new access token for the configured audience if none is cached or if the current token is expired, using an iframe-based silent authentication flow against Auth0’s domain if necessary.[6][6][29] The token returned is then attached to the Authorization header as `Bearer <token>`, matching what the FastAPI `decode_token` dependency expects. If the user lacks the required permission for `/api/messages`, the backend will respond with HTTP 403, and the frontend will display an error message.  

If `getAccessTokenSilently` returns an opaque token, this indicates that the audience configuration is wrong or missing; community discussions show that once the audience is properly configured in `Auth0Provider`, the tokens become JWTs with visible header and payload sections that the backend can decode.[15][6][26][29]  

### Using Permissions in the Frontend for UI-Level RBAC

Although security decisions must be enforced on the backend, it is often helpful to adjust the frontend UI based on whether the current user is expected to have certain permissions, for example to hide an “Admin” link for users without `write:messages`. Auth0’s community notes that access tokens are not intended to be parsed by SPAs and that if permission data is needed in the client, one should either fetch it from a backend endpoint or add it to the ID token as custom claims using Rules or Actions.[25][31][40] Nonetheless, for simple scenarios where you accept that UI-level checks are only a convenience and not a security boundary, you can decode the JWT in the frontend using a library such as `jwt-decode` and examine its `permissions` array.  

A minimal example might look like this:

```bash
npm install jwt-decode
```

```javascript
// src/hooks/usePermissions.js
import { useAuth0 } from "@auth0/auth0-react";
import jwtDecode from "jwt-decode";
import { useEffect, useState } from "react";

export function usePermissions() {
  const { isAuthenticated, getAccessTokenSilently } = useAuth0();
  const [permissions, setPermissions] = useState([]);

  useEffect(() => {
    async function loadPermissions() {
      if (!isAuthenticated) {
        setPermissions([]);
        return;
      }
      try {
        const token = await getAccessTokenSilently();
        const decoded = jwtDecode(token);
        const perms = decoded.permissions || [];
        setPermissions(perms);
      } catch (e) {
        setPermissions([]);
      }
    }
    loadPermissions();
  }, [isAuthenticated, getAccessTokenSilently]);

  return permissions;
}
```

The resulting hook can be used in components to conditionally display UI elements:

```javascript
// inside some component
import { usePermissions } from "../hooks/usePermissions";

const permissions = usePermissions();
const canWriteMessages = permissions.includes("write:messages");

return (
  <div>
    {canWriteMessages && <button>Create Message</button>}
  </div>
);
```

This pattern should always be accompanied by corresponding server-side enforcement, and documentation or comments should clearly state that frontend permission checks are advisory only.[27][31] For stricter adherence to best practices, you can instead expose a backend endpoint such as `/api/me/permissions` that returns the currently authenticated user’s permissions and roles derived directly from the token payload, and use that endpoint’s response to inform the UI, avoiding direct token parsing on the client.[31]  

## Testing Strategy for Auth0-Protected FastAPI and React Applications

### Testing FastAPI Endpoints with TestClient

FastAPI provides first-class testing support through the `TestClient` class, which wraps your application and allows you to issue HTTP requests in tests without running an external server.[21] The standard pattern involves creating a client from the FastAPI app, writing test functions that begin with `test_`, and using pytest’s assertion mechanisms to check response status codes and bodies.[21] Testing endpoints that do not require authentication is straightforward: you simply call them and assert on their responses. For RBAC-protected endpoints, you can either use real Auth0-issued tokens or mock the authentication dependencies to bypass JWT verification and simulate different permission sets.  

To test the public endpoint, you can create a `tests/test_public.py` file with the following content:

```python
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from app.app import app  # adjust import for your package structure

client = TestClient(app)

def test_public_endpoint():
    response = client.get("/api/public")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    data = response.json()
    assert "message" in data
    assert "public" in data["message"].lower()
```

This test exercises only the unauthenticated path and does not require any Auth0 configuration.[21]  

For protected endpoints, one strategy is to override the `get_current_user` dependency to return a fake user with desired permissions, thereby unit testing RBAC logic without relying on real tokens. FastAPI allows you to override dependencies in tests using the `app.dependency_overrides` dictionary.[21][41] For example, you can create a test file `tests/test_rbac.py`:

```python
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from app.app import app
from app.models import CurrentUser
from app.rbac import get_current_user

client = TestClient(app)

def override_current_user_with_read():
    return CurrentUser(sub="user123", permissions=["read:messages"])

def override_current_user_without_read():
    return CurrentUser(sub="user456", permissions=[])

def test_rbac_allows_user_with_permission():
    app.dependency_overrides[get_current_user] = override_current_user_with_read
    response = client.get("/api/messages")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    data = response.json()
    assert "You have permission to read messages" in data["message"]
    app.dependency_overrides.clear()

def test_rbac_denies_user_without_permission():
    app.dependency_overrides[get_current_user] = override_current_user_without_read
    response = client.get("/api/messages")
    assert response.status_code == 403
    data = response.json()
    assert "Permission 'read:messages' is required" in data["detail"]
    app.dependency_overrides.clear()
```

By overriding only the user retrieval dependency and leaving `require_permission` intact, these tests verify that the RBAC logic behaves correctly based on what permissions are present in the `CurrentUser` object, independent of JWT parsing.[21][27][41] This approach makes tests deterministic and fast, and eliminates external dependencies such as network requests to Auth0.  

If you want to test the full validation flow, including JWT signature verification, you can either obtain a real token from Auth0 during the test or replicate Auth0’s token structure using a symmetric algorithm and a local verifier, as suggested in some Auth0 community discussions where a test environment uses a symmetric verifier while production uses an asymmetric verifier.[33] However, such tests are more integration-oriented and can be reserved for a separate test suite or staging environment.  

### Manual API Testing with Real Auth0 Tokens

Beyond unit tests, manual testing with real Auth0-issued tokens is essential to validate that the configuration of audiences, permissions, and RBAC settings in the Auth0 dashboard aligns with the FastAPI implementation. As mentioned earlier, Auth0’s API Test tab provides a convenient way to obtain access tokens via the client credentials flow.[9][13][9][9][13] For end-user tokens, the most realistic test involves running the React SPA, logging in via Universal Login, and capturing the access token returned by `getAccessTokenSilently`, then using it in API requests.  

When testing RBAC, you can create two users in Auth0 with different roles. The first user is assigned a role containing `read:messages`, and the second is assigned a role without that permission. Using the SPA, you first log in as the user with the `read:messages` permission and invoke the `callRbacEndpoint` button, verifying that the backend responds successfully. You then log out, log in as the user without the permission, and repeat the request, verifying that the backend returns a 403 Forbidden response with the appropriate error message. This manual process validates that permissions are (a) being attached to the token by Auth0’s RBAC mechanism and (b) correctly enforced by the FastAPI permission checker.[5][2][13][18][2][19][24][13]  

If RBAC appears not to work—specifically, if the access token lacks the `permissions` array or if the permissions do not match expected roles—the most common root causes are that RBAC is not enabled for the API, that the “Add Permissions in Access Token” option is disabled, or that the SPA is requesting a token for the wrong audience, such as the Auth0 Management API’s identifier.[2][2][18][2][19][24][13] In such cases, you inspect the decoded token using a tool like `jwt.io` after extracting it from the SPA or API Test tab, confirm the `aud` claim, and adjust the Auth0 dashboard settings or the SPA’s `Auth0Provider` `authorizationParams` accordingly.[19][6][26][29]  

### Unit Testing the React SPA with Mocked Auth0

On the frontend, unit tests focus on verifying component behavior under different authentication states and on testing API-calling components with mocked access tokens. Since Auth0’s React SDK handles authentication flows externally and uses browser redirects, unit tests do not interact with real Auth0 endpoints; instead, they mock the `useAuth0` hook and provide controlled values.[34] Auth0’s tutorial on unit testing React applications protected by Auth0 demonstrates how to use Jest’s `mockReturnValue` to override `useAuth0` at the beginning of each test suite, simulating logged-in and logged-out states.[34]  

A simple Jest test file might look like this:

```javascript
// src/__mocks__/auth0-react.js
export const useAuth0 = jest.fn();
export const Auth0Provider = ({ children }) => <div>{children}</div>;
```

```javascript
// src/components/__tests__/LoginButton.test.js
import React from "react";
import { render, screen, fireEvent } from "@testing-library/react";
import { useAuth0 } from "@auth0/auth0-react";
import { LoginButton } from "../LoginButton";

jest.mock("@auth0/auth0-react");

test("renders login button when not authenticated", () => {
  useAuth0.mockReturnValue({
    isAuthenticated: false,
    loginWithRedirect: jest.fn(),
  });

  render(<LoginButton />);
  expect(screen.getByText(/log in/i)).toBeInTheDocument();
});

test("does not render login button when authenticated", () => {
  useAuth0.mockReturnValue({
    isAuthenticated: true,
    loginWithRedirect: jest.fn(),
  });

  render(<LoginButton />);
  expect(screen.queryByText(/log in/i)).toBeNull();
});
```

Auth0’s unit testing article extends this pattern to test the logout button and profile components by providing a mock `user` object and `isAuthenticated` flag, verifying that the correct UI is rendered and that click events call the expected Auth0 SDK functions.[34] Because `Auth0Provider` is mocked to simply render its children, tests remain isolated from Auth0’s real behavior and focus on your application logic.  

For components that call the FastAPI backend using `getAccessTokenSilently`, you can mock `getAccessTokenSilently` to return a static string or reject with an error, and you can mock `fetch` or use libraries such as `msw` to simulate API responses. This allows you to verify that the component correctly handles success and error states when interacting with the API, without requiring a running FastAPI server during unit tests.  

### End-to-End Testing Considerations

While unit and integration tests for the frontend and backend separately provide confidence in local logic, full end-to-end (E2E) tests are often required to validate that the entire system—React SPA, Auth0, and FastAPI API—work together as expected. Such tests typically run a real FastAPI instance and React dev server or statically built SPA, connect to an Auth0 test tenant, and use Cypress, Playwright, or similar tools to automate browser interactions.  

In a typical E2E scenario, the test script navigates to the React SPA, clicks the login button, interacts with Auth0’s Universal Login page to authenticate with a known test user (either using Auth0’s database connection or Google social login in a headless browser), and then calls protected API endpoints through the SPA, verifying that RBAC is correctly enforced based on the test user’s assigned roles and permissions.[6][8][13][18][24][13][34] While setting up such E2E tests requires additional infrastructure for secrets management and test isolation, they provide the highest level of assurance that the entire integration—including redirect URLs, CORS settings, RBAC configuration, and token validation—is correct.  

## Security Best Practices and Common Pitfalls

### Protecting Secrets and Configuration

Throughout the integration, careful handling of secrets and configuration is essential. Auth0 strongly recommends using RS256 as the JWT signing algorithm, which eliminates the need for your FastAPI backend to manage private keys; it only needs the public JWKS that Auth0 exposes.[6][9][13][9][9][23][13] Any Auth0 client secrets, such as those used for machine-to-machine clients or custom scripts, must be stored exclusively on trusted backend servers or secure configuration stores, never in browser code or version control. The React SPA, being a public client, should store only non-secret values such as the Domain and Client ID in its `.env` files, which are ultimately embedded in the client bundle but pose no security risk in themselves.[6][6][6][26][29]  

In FastAPI, the `AUTH0_DOMAIN` and `AUTH0_AUDIENCE` values should be configured using environment variables loaded from `.env` during development and from environment configuration in production. Do not commit `.env` files containing sensitive values to version control; instead, provide sample configuration templates without secrets. If your FastAPI API also uses client credentials to call Auth0’s Management API, for example to assign roles programmatically, those client IDs and secrets should be stored securely and never exposed to the SPA.[18][20][30][40]  

### Validating Tokens Correctly

When validating JWTs, it is critical to verify not only the signature but also the issuer and audience claims, and to respect expiration times. Auth0’s JWT handling guides show that decoding a token with PyJWT or `python-jose` is straightforward, but they emphasize the need to provide the correct key, algorithm list, audience, and issuer to `jwt.decode` to ensure that a token has not been tampered with or misissued.[23][32] If you omit audience or issuer checks, your API may inadvertently accept tokens intended for other APIs or tenants.  

In the presented `decode_token` function, the `jwt.decode` call passes `audience=settings.auth0_audience` and `issuer=settings.auth0_issuer`, and it restricts algorithms to RS256. These parameters ensure that only tokens issued by your Auth0 tenant for your specific API will be accepted.[9][13][9][9][23][13][32] Access tokens must also be checked for expiration; `python-jose` will raise an `ExpiredSignatureError` when `exp` is in the past, which you catch and convert into a 401 Unauthorized error, instructing clients to re-authenticate or refresh tokens.[23][32]  

A subtle but important pitfall arises when the SPA requests tokens without specifying the audience, or with the wrong audience, leading to tokens whose `aud` claim does not match the FastAPI API. In such cases, your decoder will raise `JWTClaimsError` or similar, and the `permissions` claim will likely be absent or correspond to a different API. Community reports show that passing the correct custom API identifier as the audience in the login request is necessary to receive the expected permissions, and once corrected, the permissions appear “out of the box.”[15][19][6][26][29]  

### Scopes vs Permissions and RBAC Configuration Pitfalls

Confusion between OAuth scopes and Auth0 permissions is a frequent source of integration issues. Scopes are strings that limit what an application can do on behalf of a user, and they appear in the `scope` claim of a token. Permissions, in Auth0’s core RBAC model, are specific to an API and appear in the `permissions` array when RBAC is enabled and configured to add permissions to the access token.[2][5][2][12][18][2][19][24][25][29] Enabling RBAC for an API and selecting “Add Permissions in the Access Token” ensures that your FastAPI API receives the `permissions` claim without needing to ask for specific scopes in the authorize request.[2][2][2][24]  

In first-party scenarios where the SPA and backend are controlled by the same organization, community advice suggests that permissions are more appropriate than scopes for defining user capabilities, since the backend usually trusts the frontend and the emphasis is on user privileges rather than delegated application privileges.[2][2][12][2][24] Nonetheless, if you request specific API scopes via the SPA’s `Auth0Provider` configuration, those scopes will appear in the `scope` claim, and Auth0’s core RBAC will restrict those scopes to the ones granted by the user’s permissions and roles.[5][2][12][25][29]  

Common misconfigurations include failing to enable RBAC on the API, failing to select “Add Permissions in Access Token,” or forgetting to enable the permissions to roles mapping such that roles contain no effective permissions.[2][5][2][12][18][2][19][24] Another frequent issue is assigning roles but then testing with a token whose audience points to the Auth0 Management API or another resource server, in which case the token’s permissions reflect that API instead of your custom API.[19] Resolving these issues typically involves carefully reviewing the API settings in Auth0’s dashboard, verifying the audience in the SPA’s `Auth0Provider` configuration, and checking the decoded token to confirm the presence and values of the `permissions` claim.  

### Universal Login and Connection Configuration Pitfalls

Universal Login simplifies authentication but introduces another set of configuration axes: database connections, social connections, default directories, and login experience options. If you observe that username/password fields appear in the Universal Login page even when you have disabled the database connection for a particular application, the issue may be related to the tenant’s Default Directory setting, which can cause the Resource Owner Password flow and Universal Login to use a specific connection if configured.[17][28] Ensuring that the Default Directory is either unset or set to a connection you intend to use, and verifying that each connection is enabled or disabled for the correct applications, helps prevent such discrepancies.[17][28]  

For Google social login, misconfigured authorized domains or redirect URIs in Google Cloud are a common source of errors. If the authorized JavaScript origin does not match your Auth0 Domain exactly, or if the redirect URI is not set to the correct Auth0 `/login/callback` URL, Google will reject the authorization request.[8] Similarly, in Auth0’s social connection configuration, the Google client ID and secret must exactly match the credentials that Google created for your project.[8]  

When using New Universal Login, CSS customization is limited, and only the logo and some text content can be changed easily; attempts to apply arbitrary CSS across all login pages are not supported in the same way they were in Classic login.[4][7][22] For customizing text on pages such as password reset, you can use Management API endpoints like `PUT /api/v2/prompts/reset-password/custom-text/en` to adjust titles and descriptions, but the underlying layout remains managed by Auth0.[4][7][22]  

### Multi-Environment Configuration and Tenant Strategy

For production-grade deployments, you will likely maintain separate environments such as development, staging, and production. The question of whether to use multiple Auth0 tenants or a single tenant with multiple applications and APIs is common. While Auth0 does not mandate a specific strategy, many practitioners choose separate tenants for clear isolation of configuration, data, and user accounts, especially between development/test and production.[35]   

If you adopt multiple tenants, you will maintain separate Domain, Client ID, and Audience values for each environment and configure your React and FastAPI applications via environment-specific configuration files or variables. For example, `AUTH0_DOMAIN` and `AUTH0_AUDIENCE` differ across environments, and you may maintain separate `.env.development` and `.env.production` files in the React project. Auth0’s CLI and APIs can help automate the creation and synchronization of configuration across tenants, but the essential point for the integration playbook is that all three components—React, Auth0, and FastAPI—must agree on these values per environment.[35]  

### Token Usage Boundaries and Client-Side Parsing

Finally, it is important to respect the boundaries of token usage. Access tokens are meant to be consumed by resource servers and not by SPAs, whereas ID tokens are intended for clients to understand identity context.[6][25][29][31] Auth0’s community guidelines recommend that if you require permission information in the client, you either fetch it from a backend endpoint or add it as custom claims to the ID token via Actions or Rules, instead of directly parsing the access token.[25][31][40]  

In the present integration, the React SPA does not need to parse the access token to function correctly; it only needs to call `getAccessTokenSilently` and attach the token as a Bearer credential to backend requests. Any optional client-side parsing for UI-level RBAC must be understood as advisory and should never replace server-side enforcement. By keeping security-critical logic in the FastAPI API and using Auth0’s signed tokens as the single source of truth for permissions and roles, you reduce the risk of client-side tampering and maintain a clear trust boundary.[27][31][32][41]  

## Conclusion

This integration playbook has presented a comprehensive, technically detailed guide for securing a FastAPI backend and a React frontend with Auth0’s New Universal Login and Role-Based Access Control based on permissions embedded in access tokens. The narrative began by establishing a conceptual understanding of the roles of Auth0, FastAPI, and React in an OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect architecture, emphasizing that Auth0 handles authentication and authorization flows, FastAPI acts as the resource server that validates access tokens and enforces RBAC, and React, through the Auth0 React SDK, acts as a public client that delegates all credential handling to Universal Login.[6][7][9][13][6][9][20][6][26][29][13]  

On the Auth0 side, the playbook described the creation and configuration of an SPA application and a FastAPI API, including setting callback, logout, and web origin URLs; defining a unique API identifier used as the audience for access tokens; and enabling RS256 signing. It explained how to configure an email–password database connection and a Google social connection so that New Universal Login can present both login options without changes to React or FastAPI code.[6][7][8][17][22] It then explored Auth0’s core RBAC model: defining API permissions, creating roles that group permissions, assigning roles to users, and enabling RBAC and “Add Permissions in Access Token” so that permissions appear in the access token’s `permissions` claim.[2][5][2][12][13][18][2][19][24][13]  

On the FastAPI side, the playbook implemented robust JWT validation using `python-jose` and Auth0’s JWKS endpoint, demonstrating how to extract the Bearer token from the Authorization header, decode the unverified header to obtain the key ID, fetch and select the appropriate JWK, and call `jwt.decode` with the correct public key, algorithms, audience, and issuer to validate tokens.[9][9][9][23][13][32] A Pydantic `CurrentUser` model and `get_current_user` dependency were introduced to wrap the decoded token payload, and a `require_permission` dependency was implemented to enforce RBAC by checking for required permissions in the token’s `permissions` array. These elements were woven into a FastAPI app with public, authenticated, and permission-protected endpoints, illustrating how to apply RBAC in practice and how to configure CORS for the React SPA.[27][41]  

On the React side, the playbook guided the installation and configuration of the Auth0 React SDK, the creation of environment variables for `REACT_APP_AUTH0_DOMAIN`, `REACT_APP_AUTH0_CLIENT_ID`, and `REACT_APP_AUTH0_AUDIENCE`, and the integration of `Auth0Provider` at the root of the application, including specifying the API audience in `authorizationParams` to ensure that access tokens are JWTs usable by FastAPI.[6][15][6][26][29][34] It demonstrated how to implement login and logout buttons using `loginWithRedirect` and `logout`, how to create profile components using `user` from `useAuth0`, and how to guard routes with `withAuthenticationRequired`. It then showed how to call the FastAPI API using `getAccessTokenSilently` to obtain access tokens and attach them as Bearer tokens in fetch requests, and discussed optional client-side permission parsing for UI-level RBAC, with an emphasis that actual authorization remains server-side.[6][6][26][29][31][34]  

The testing section outlined a strategy for unit testing FastAPI endpoints using `TestClient`, including overriding authentication dependencies to simulate users with different permissions and verifying that RBAC enforcement behaves as expected. It suggested manual integration tests using real Auth0 tokens to validate that RBAC configuration in the Auth0 dashboard matches backend expectations, and it covered unit testing of React components by mocking the `useAuth0` hook and verifying component behavior in authenticated and unauthenticated states, as recommended by Auth0’s unit testing guidance.[21][13][33][34][41] For higher assurance, it briefly discussed end-to-end testing approaches using browser automation to cover the full React–Auth0–FastAPI flow.  

Finally, the playbook addressed security best practices and common pitfalls, stressing careful secret management, proper validation of issuer and audience claims, and adherence to RS256 for token signing.[6][9][13][9][9][23][13][32] It clarified the distinction between scopes and permissions, and emphasized the importance of enabling RBAC and including permissions in access tokens when implementing permission-based authorization. It outlined Universal Login and connection configuration pitfalls, such as misconfigured Default Directory or Google OAuth settings, and touched on multi-environment strategies involving separate Auth0 tenants for development, staging, and production.[2][5][8][12][17][18][2][19][24][28][35]  

Taken together, these elements form a coherent, step-by-step integration playbook that a developer can follow to construct a secure, RBAC-enabled FastAPI + React application using Auth0’s New Universal Login with email–password and Google login. By aligning Auth0 configuration, FastAPI token validation and permission enforcement, and React’s Auth0 SDK usage, and by rigorously testing each piece, you can deploy a robust authentication and authorization solution that leverages industry-standard identity protocols without reinventing them yourself.[6][7][9][13][6][9][20][6][26][29][13]

message.content:
# Integration Playbook: Auth0 New Universal Login with RBAC in a FastAPI + React Application

This report presents a complete, end-to-end integration playbook for securing a modern single-page application stack composed of a React frontend and a FastAPI backend using Auth0’s New Universal Login, email–password database authentication, Google social login, and Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) implemented via permissions embedded in Auth0 access tokens. The playbook describes how to configure Auth0 tenants, applications, APIs, database and social connections, and RBAC policies; how to wire a FastAPI service to validate Auth0-issued JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) and enforce permission-based access to endpoints; how to connect a React SPA using the Auth0 React SDK so that authentication flows are handled through Universal Login and access tokens are obtained transparently under the Authorization Code Flow with PKCE; and how to test and harden this integration. Throughout, it emphasizes secure handling of configuration, the correct use of audiences, scopes, and permissions, and common pitfalls such as opaque tokens, missing RBAC configuration, or misaligned callback URLs, providing complete code samples and detailed testing strategies grounded in Auth0’s official documentation and community best practices.[5][6][9][13][6][9][6][26][29][13]  

## Conceptual and Architectural Overview

A successful integration of Auth0 with a FastAPI backend and a React frontend begins with a clear understanding of the responsibility boundaries among the three components and of the OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect flows that Auth0 orchestrates on their behalf.[6][7][20][29] Auth0 acts as the identity provider and authorization server: it authenticates users, manages connections to underlying identity stores such as an Auth0-hosted database or Google’s OAuth 2.0 endpoint, issues ID and access tokens, and enforces RBAC policies based on roles and permissions configured in the tenant.[5][8][17][18] The React application is a public client that never holds server-side secrets and delegates all login and logout flows to Auth0’s New Universal Login through browser redirects; it receives tokens via redirects under the Authorization Code Flow with PKCE implemented by the Auth0 React SDK.[6][6][6][26][29] The FastAPI service is the resource server, or API, that receives access tokens from the React client in the Authorization header, validates their signature and claims, and uses the permissions claim that Auth0 embeds in the token when RBAC is enabled to decide whether to authorize access to particular operations.[5][2][13][2][19][25][29]  

In this architecture, the ID token is primarily intended for the SPA to understand who the user is and to personalize the interface, while the access token is aimed at the FastAPI backend and is the only token that should be trusted to make authorization decisions for protected resources.[6][25][29][31] The Auth0 React SDK encapsulates the complexity of PKCE, token storage, token renewal, and Universal Login invocation, exposing simple React hooks such as `useAuth0` with methods like `loginWithRedirect`, `logout`, and `getAccessTokenSilently` to the application developer.[6][6][29][34] On the backend, JWT validation can be implemented either using Auth0’s `auth0-fastapi-api` helper library, as promoted by the FastAPI quickstart, or manually through a standard JWT library such as `python-jose` or PyJWT, using the JSON Web Key Set (JWKS) that Auth0 publishes at a well-known URL.[9][13][9][23][13][32]  

RBAC in Auth0 is implemented by defining API-level permissions, grouping them into roles, and assigning roles to users, with optional augmentation of authorization decisions using Auth0 Actions for context-sensitive policies.[5][18][25][40] When core RBAC is enabled for an API, and the option to “Add Permissions in the Access Token” is selected, Auth0 will automatically include a `permissions` array in the access token for that API, containing the specific permissions granted to the user based on their roles.[2][5][2][13][18][2][19][24][13] This array is the foundation for enforcing permission-based authorization in FastAPI: endpoints require particular permissions, and a shared dependency in FastAPI inspects the decoded token’s `permissions` claim to accept or reject requests. At the same time, the React application can optionally inspect permissions or roles to adapt the user interface, although the security boundary always remains at the backend.[27][29][31][41]  

Within Auth0, the New Universal Login Experience centralizes the login pages and removes the need for the React application to host or maintain its own login forms.[6][7][22] Instead, the React app redirects users to Auth0’s Universal Login domain, which can be configured to use an Auth0-hosted user database for email–password authentication and a Google social connection for federated login, and to present a consistent brand appearance.[6][7][8][17][22] The ability to add database connections and social connections, define actions that run during login, and configure advanced Universal Login behaviors such as identifier-first flows or passwordless login provides flexibility without complicating the React and FastAPI codebases.[7][8][17][37]  

The remainder of this report proceeds in a stepwise but narrative fashion. It first describes the configuration of the Auth0 tenant, applications, APIs, connections, and RBAC roles to support New Universal Login with both email–password and Google login. It then develops a FastAPI backend that validates Auth0 JWTs, configures CORS for the SPA, and enforces RBAC via permissions. Next, it presents a React SPA that uses the Auth0 React SDK for authentication and securely calls the FastAPI API. Finally, it discusses testing patterns, security best practices, and common pitfalls, yielding a complete integration playbook for developers building production-grade Auth0-secured FastAPI and React systems.  

## Auth0 Tenant, Applications, API, Connections, and RBAC Configuration

### Creating the Auth0 Tenant and React Application

To begin, you must have an Auth0 tenant that represents the product or environment for which you are adding authentication.[6][6][34] When you sign up for Auth0, you choose a tenant name that is combined with an Auth0 regional domain to form the Auth0 domain, such as `your-tenant.us.auth0.com` or `your-tenant.eu.auth0.com`; this domain is the base URL through which both your React application and FastAPI backend will communicate with Auth0’s APIs and Universal Login pages.[6][6][6] Within this tenant, the first fundamental object is the Auth0 application corresponding to the React SPA. In the Auth0 dashboard, you navigate to the Applications section, choose to create a new application, and select a Single Page Application type such that the correct OAuth 2.0 flows and security settings are applied.[6][6][6][26][29][34]  

Once the SPA application is created, its Settings tab exposes key configuration fields that must align with your React app’s runtime URLs to make the OAuth redirect flows work correctly. The Allowed Callback URLs field must include the React app’s URL where Auth0 should redirect users after successful authentication; for a local development environment using a standard React dev server this will typically be `http://localhost:3000`, while Auth0’s own React guides often assume `http://localhost:4040` for examples.[6][6][6][26][34] The Allowed Logout URLs field must include the URL where users land after logging out, often set to the same origin as the callback URL so that `window.location.origin` can be used in the React `logout` call. The Allowed Web Origins field must include the SPA’s origin to permit silent token renewal and CORS requests from the browser to Auth0’s domain.[6][6][6][26][34] Failure to correctly configure these URLs commonly manifests as redirect errors or blank pages during login and logout.  

In addition to redirect URLs, the SPA Settings page displays two values that the React client must know: the Domain and the Client ID. The Domain is the Auth0 tenant’s domain created earlier, and the Client ID is a non-secret public identifier that uniquely identifies this SPA application and cannot be changed.[6][6][6][26][29][34] Within the React project you will typically store these values in environment variables such as `REACT_APP_AUTH0_DOMAIN` and `REACT_APP_AUTH0_CLIENT_ID`, and use them to configure the Auth0 React SDK’s `Auth0Provider` component.[6][6][6][26][29] Because the React app is a public client, it never stores a client secret; all sensitive operations are performed on Auth0’s backend and on your FastAPI backend, not in the browser.[6][20][29]  

At this stage, the SPA application is registered but not yet connected to an API, and no RBAC configuration has been enabled. However, it is already capable of driving a basic authentication experience using Auth0’s New Universal Login once you enable a database connection or a social connection and integrate the Auth0 React SDK in the frontend.[6][7][6][6][22][29] The precise connection setup and RBAC configuration are described in subsequent subsections.  

### Creating the FastAPI API in Auth0 and Defining Permissions

The second fundamental Auth0 object is the API that represents the FastAPI backend in Auth0’s authorization domain. In the Auth0 dashboard, you open the APIs section and create a new API entry for your FastAPI service, providing a human-readable Name and a unique Identifier that will serve as the `aud` (audience) claim for access tokens issued for this API.[9][13][9][9][24][13] Auth0 documentation recommends using a URL-like string as the identifier, such as `https://my-fastapi-api`, although it need not correspond to a publicly accessible URL.[9][13][9][9][13] The Signing Algorithm for this new API should be left at the default of RS256, which uses an asymmetric public–private key pair and is recommended by Auth0 for security and scalability; the private key remains with Auth0, and the public key is exposed via JWKS for your FastAPI service to validate signatures.[6][9][13][9][9][23][13]  

Once the API is created, Auth0’s quickstart for FastAPI recommends taking note of two values: the Auth0 Domain and the API’s Identifier, which together form the issuer and audience values used when validating access tokens in the FastAPI application and are typically stored as environment variables such as `AUTH0_DOMAIN` and `AUTH0_AUDIENCE`.[9][13][9][9][13] The `AUTH0_DOMAIN` will match the SPA’s domain, while the `AUTH0_AUDIENCE` corresponds to the API Identifier you just configured, for example `https://my-fastapi-api`. This alignment is critical: when the React application later requests an access token, it must pass the same audience value to Auth0 so that Auth0 issues a token targeted at this API; otherwise, the backend will either receive an opaque token or a token whose `aud` claim points to a different API, such as the Auth0 Management API, causing permission mismatches and authorization failures.[15][19][6][26][29]  

With the API entry created, you define the fine-grained API permissions that embody your RBAC policy. In the API’s Permissions tab, you add named permissions, also known as API scopes in Auth0, such as `read:messages` or `write:messages` with descriptive text explaining their purpose.[13][25][13] These permissions are later mapped to roles and then assigned to users, acting as atomic units of authorization. Auth0’s documentation recommends that permissions be relatively granular so that roles can combine them flexibly, though the ideal granularity depends on your domain.[5][24][25] The important point for the integration is that these permissions names will appear in the access token’s `permissions` array when RBAC is enabled and will be checked by FastAPI code at runtime.  

Enabling RBAC requires a specific configuration step on the API’s Settings page. Within this page, you enable the option for Role-Based Access Control for this API and, crucially, select the additional option to add permissions to the access token such that Auth0 includes the `permissions` claim in the token payload for tokens issued for this API.[2][5][2][13][18][2][19][24][13] Community guidance emphasizes that this setting is what differentiates permissions from scopes and ensures that backend APIs can inspect the `permissions` array without needing to make extra calls to the Auth0 Management API.[2][2][2][19][24] Once RBAC and “Add Permissions in Access Token” are enabled, any user who has a role containing API permissions and who obtains a token for this API will receive those permissions in the access token.  

### Configuring Roles and Assigning Them to Users

Auth0’s RBAC model centers on roles that group permissions, which can then be assigned to users, allowing administrators to easily adjust access rights by changing role assignments instead of altering individual permissions for each user.[5][12][18][24] Within the Auth0 dashboard, you can create roles under the Roles section, for example defining an `admin` role with both `read:messages` and `write:messages` permissions and a `reader` role that only grants `read:messages`. Once roles are created, you assign them to users either manually through the User Management interface or programmatically through the Management API.[5][12][18][24][30]  

When assigning roles through the dashboard, you locate a user in the Users list, open either a context menu to assign roles or navigate to the user’s profile page and use the Roles tab to attach one or more roles.[18] If your deployment requires many roles or automated provisioning, you can call the Management API’s Assign User Roles endpoint with a suitable access token that has `read:roles` and `update:users` scopes, passing the user’s ID and a list of role IDs.[18][30] For many first-party applications with relatively simple RBAC, however, dashboard-based assignment is sufficient, and the roles will automatically be reflected in tokens once the user authenticates and requests tokens for the relevant API, assuming RBAC is enabled and the scopes are requested or implied.[5][2][18][2][24]  

It is important to note the conceptual distinction between scopes and permissions in Auth0’s model. Scopes remain an OAuth 2.0 concept used primarily to constrain what a client application can do on behalf of a user, and they are expressed in tokens as a space-separated `scope` string claim. Permissions, by contrast, are Auth0’s API-specific authorization abstraction and appear in the `permissions` array claim when RBAC is enabled.[2][5][2][2][6][25][29] In first-party scenarios where a React SPA invokes a backend that is under the same control as the SPA, it is common and recommended to rely on permissions and roles instead of implementing complex client-side scope-based delegation, because the backend trusts the SPA and the main question is what the user is allowed to do, not what the client is allowed to do on the user’s behalf.[2][5][2][2][24]  

If your application needs more context-aware authorization than roles and permissions alone can express, Auth0 Actions can augment the RBAC decision with attribute-based rules. For example, you can write an Action for the Login / Post Login trigger that denies access on weekends for a particular application, checks IP ranges for corporate network access, or rejects all access to a specific API audience, using the `api.access.deny` method with custom messages.[40] Actions can also add custom claims representing user metadata or derived roles into ID or access tokens using methods like `api.idToken.setCustomClaim` and `api.accessToken.setCustomClaim`, allowing more complex claims to be available for decision-making in your FastAPI backend.[25][40] For the present integration playbook, however, the core RBAC mechanism of permissions in the access token suffices to implement robust role-based authorization.  

### Enabling Auth0 New Universal Login

With the SPA and API created and RBAC defined, the next step is to configure Auth0’s New Universal Login Experience. Universal Login centralizes login, signup, password reset, and related flows on Auth0-hosted pages, reducing the attack surface and maintenance burden on your own applications, and supports additional features such as social login, passwordless flows, and multi-factor authentication without code changes in your React app.[6][7][17][22]  

In the Auth0 dashboard, you navigate to Branding, then Universal Login, and select the New experience as your default login experience.[6][7][6][22] This choice instructs Auth0 to use its modern, identifier-first-capable pages instead of the older Classic login templates. For most standard React SPA integrations, New Universal Login is recommended, and it is also required for certain features such as the built-in Signup support used by Auth0’s React guides.[6][7][6] The New Universal Login offers limited CSS customization, allowing you to brand your application with a custom logo and some text customizations, but not arbitrary CSS across all elements; for more extensive theming you can use text customization endpoints or, for some flows like password reset, Management API endpoints to change specific text content.[4][7][22]  

Once Universal Login is active, your React application no longer needs to render login forms or handle user credentials directly. Instead, it redirects users to the Auth0 domain’s `/authorize` endpoint with parameters such as the SPA’s client ID, the requested response type, redirect URI, and, crucially, the audience and scopes when requesting API access.[6][7][6][26][29] The Auth0 React SDK handles the construction of these URLs under the hood, invoking Universal Login and managing the state parameter that protects against CSRF attacks.[6][6][26][29] After the user authenticates with either email–password or Google and consents to any requested scopes, Auth0 redirects back to the SPA, which uses the SDK to parse the callback and establish a session backed by ID and access tokens stored in memory or browser storage according to the SDK’s design.[6][6][26][29]  

If your organization requires identifier-first authentication, where users enter their email first and are then routed to the correct corporate or database login method, Auth0 provides an Authentication Profile setting that allows switching the flow to identifier-first or identifier-first with WebAuthn biometrics, though the underlying SPA integration remains the same: Universal Login is invoked and handles the multi-step flow.[37] For this playbook’s scenario, a standard email–password plus Google login under New Universal Login is sufficient.  

### Configuring Email–Password Database Connection

To support email–password login with Auth0-hosted user storage, you configure a database connection. Database connections in Auth0 represent user stores where users authenticate with an identifier such as email or username and a password or passkey, with credentials stored either in Auth0’s own user store or in a custom database that you control.[17] For typical SaaS applications starting from scratch, using Auth0’s standard database is easiest and offers optimal performance because all authentication data and logic are managed by Auth0.[17]  

In the Auth0 dashboard, you navigate to Authentication and then Database, where you can create a new database connection or use the default `Username-Password-Authentication` connection. When you create or edit a database connection, you can configure whether signups are allowed, email verification behavior, password policy, and whether to use custom database scripts to talk to your own backend.[17] For the integration here, you can use the standard Auth0 database with email as the identifier and no custom scripts, allowing Auth0 to take full responsibility for credential storage and validation.  

Crucially, a database connection must be enabled for the specific SPA application you created earlier. Within the database connection’s Applications tab, you ensure that the SPA is toggled on; otherwise, even if the connection exists, users will not see the email–password login form when accessing your SPA’s Universal Login page.[6][17] In addition, if you want Universal Login or resource owner password flows to default to this database connection, you verify the Default Directory setting under the tenant’s advanced settings, which should match the name of the database connection when appropriate.[28] If the database connection is disabled or incorrectly configured, you may encounter situations where username/password fields appear unexpectedly or not at all, or where enabling or disabling the connection for an application does not immediately reflect in the Universal Login UI due to the Default Directory behavior under some conditions.[28]  

For advanced scenarios in which your users are stored in an existing enterprise database or in a legacy user store, Auth0’s custom database feature allows you to write Node.js-based scripts for login, signup, password reset, and email verification, connecting to databases such as MongoDB, MySQL, or SQL Server or to external web services through Basic Auth.[17] However, these complexities are not necessary for a greenfield FastAPI + React SPA and are therefore beyond the scope of this playbook.  

### Configuring Google Social Login

To support login with Google, you configure a social connection in Auth0 and connect it to Google’s OAuth 2.0 endpoints. Auth0’s Google social connection relies on an OAuth client ID and secret created within a Google Cloud project, which identify your Auth0 tenant as an OAuth client to Google’s authorization servers.[8] Before configuring the connection in Auth0, you therefore create a Google Cloud project, configure an OAuth consent screen, register authorized domains, and create OAuth credentials of type Web Application.[8]  

Within the Google API Console, you create a project and then configure the OAuth consent screen, selecting “External” user type for consumer-facing applications unless you are within a Google Workspace environment where “Internal” applies.[8] During this process, you set the application name, support email, and authorized domains, adding `auth0.com` as an authorized domain so that Google will allow redirects to Auth0’s domain.[8] Then, in the Credentials section, you create an OAuth client ID of type Web Application, providing a name and specifying authorized JavaScript origins and redirect URIs. The authorized JavaScript origin is your Auth0 domain with `https://` and no path, for example `https://your-tenant.us.auth0.com`; the authorized redirect URI is the Auth0 login callback endpoint at `https://your-tenant.us.auth0.com/login/callback`.[8] Once these values are saved, Google generates a client ID and client secret that you will paste into Auth0.  

In the Auth0 dashboard, you navigate to Authentication and then Social, select the Google OAuth2 connection, and configure it with the client ID and client secret from your Google Cloud project, enabling it for your SPA application.[8] After you save the settings, Google login will appear as an option on your New Universal Login page for the SPA, allowing users to authenticate with their Google accounts. Because Universal Login fully handles the OAuth protocol, neither your React app nor your FastAPI backend needs to implement any direct communication with Google’s APIs.[6][7][8][29]  

### Scopes, Permissions, and Token Content

With the SPA, API, database connection, social connection, and RBAC configuration in place, it is useful to understand what tokens Auth0 will issue and what those tokens will contain. When the React SPA uses the Auth0 React SDK to initiate login and requests both an ID token for authentication and an access token for calling the FastAPI API, Auth0 will apply OpenID Connect and OAuth concepts to issue the appropriate tokens.[6][6][20][6][25][29]  

For authentication, the SPA requests at least the `openid` scope, and typically also `profile` and `email`, causing the ID token to contain core identity information such as the user’s subject (`sub`) identifier, name, nickname, picture, and email attributes according to the OpenID Connect standard.[6][25][29] The React application uses the ID token indirectly through the Auth0 React SDK, which exposes a `user` object via `useAuth0` with fields for name, email, and profile picture, but the ID token is not intended to be consumed by the FastAPI backend.[6][6][29] For API authorization, the SPA requests an access token that includes scopes corresponding to API permissions and a `permissions` array when RBAC is enabled, providing the FastAPI backend with granular information about what the user is authorized to do.[2][5][2][13][2][19][6][25][29][13]  

The token format depends on whether an audience is specified. If the SPA does not specify an API audience when calling Auth0, Auth0 may issue an opaque access token that is only meaningful to Auth0 and cannot be validated by your FastAPI service.[15][6][26][29] When you configure the Auth0 React SDK’s `Auth0Provider` to use an `authorizationParams` object that includes the `audience` equal to the FastAPI API identifier, Auth0 instead issues a JWT access token containing claims including `iss` (issuer, the Auth0 domain), `sub` (user ID), `aud` (audience, the API identifier), `exp` and `iat` timestamps, and application-specific claims such as `scope` and `permissions`.[15][19][6][26][29] The `scope` claim is a space-delimited string of granted scopes for the API, while `permissions` is an array of permission names when RBAC and “Add permissions in access token” are configured.[2][5][2][13][2][19][24][13]  

Auth0’s documentation and community resources emphasize that access tokens are designed to be consumed by resource servers, not by SPAs, and therefore the definitive enforcement of permissions must occur in the FastAPI backend, not in the React app.[31] If you require permission data in the frontend to hide or show user interface elements for convenience, you can either make an API call to the FastAPI backend to retrieve permitted actions, or you can add those permissions as custom claims to the ID token using Actions or Rules by querying the Management API for user permissions during login.[25][31][40] In the present design, however, the SPA primarily needs the access token as an opaque credential to send to the API, and the RBAC enforcement occurs exclusively on the server side.  

## Implementing the FastAPI Backend with Auth0 and RBAC

### Project Setup and Dependencies

The FastAPI application acts as the protected API that will validate Auth0-issued JWTs and enforce RBAC based on permissions in the access token’s payload. Auth0’s quickstart for FastAPI assumes Python 3.9 or higher and the `pip` package manager, and recommends creating a virtual environment and installing FastAPI, Uvicorn, the `auth0-fastapi-api` helper library, and a utility for loading environment variables such as `python-dotenv`.[9][13][9][9][13] You can follow this pattern by creating a project directory, initializing a virtual environment, and specifying a `requirements.txt` file. The commands for a Unix-like system resemble the following, which should be executed in a terminal from the directory where you want the project to live:

```bash
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate

cat > requirements.txt << 'EOF'
fastapi>=0.115.0
uvicorn[standard]>=0.34.0
python-dotenv>=1.0.0
python-jose[cryptography]>=3.3.0
requests>=2.31.0
EOF

pip install -r requirements.txt
```

Auth0’s own quickstart adds `auth0-fastapi-api>=1.0.0b5` to the dependencies; you may choose to include this library as well if you prefer to rely on its helpers for token validation and user extraction instead of writing your own JWT validation logic, but the code samples in this playbook demonstrate manual validation using `python-jose` and HTTP requests to Auth0’s JWKS endpoint, both to keep the dependencies minimal and to make the underlying mechanics explicit.[9][13][9][9][13][32]  

The `python-jose` library provides a `jwt.decode` function that both verifies the token’s signature and validates reserved claims by default, aligning with Auth0’s guidance that decode functions additionally verify tokens when properly configured.[10][23][32] The `requests` library is used to retrieve the JWKS from Auth0’s `.well-known/jwks.json` endpoint. FastAPI’s own testing tools rely on `httpx`, but that is not needed for basic runtime behavior and can be added later when writing tests.[21][41]  

### Environment Configuration and Settings

For both security and portability, you should avoid hardcoding Auth0 configuration values into your application source code. Instead, you store the Auth0 domain and the FastAPI API’s audience (identifier) in environment variables, which can be loaded from a `.env` file during development using `python-dotenv` and supplied via environment configuration in staging and production. Auth0’s Quickstart for FastAPI and its React authentication guides both follow this pattern, naming environment variables consistently so that backend and frontend share the same audience and domain values.[9][13][9][6][9][26][13]  

At the root of your FastAPI project, create a `.env` file with content resembling:

```ini
AUTH0_DOMAIN=your-tenant.us.auth0.com
AUTH0_AUDIENCE=https://my-fastapi-api
```

Here, `AUTH0_DOMAIN` should exactly match the Domain of your Auth0 tenant, obtainable from the Application or API Settings tab or from the Test tab’s cURL examples, and `AUTH0_AUDIENCE` should match the Identifier of the API you created for the FastAPI service.[9][13][9][6][9][26][13] These values form the issuer and audience claims that your JWT validation logic must verify in tokens received from clients. Later, the React SPA will be configured with the same audience to ensure that the access tokens it requests are intended for this API, and many common RBAC misconfigurations stem from mismatched audiences where the token’s `aud` claim points to the wrong resource server, such as the Auth0 Management API instead of your custom API.[19][6][26][29]  

In your application code, you can create a small configuration module that reads these environment variables and exposes them via a configuration object. For example, you might create a `config.py` file with the following content:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

class Settings:
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self.auth0_domain = os.environ["AUTH0_DOMAIN"]
        self.auth0_audience = os.environ["AUTH0_AUDIENCE"]
        self.auth0_issuer = f"https://{self.auth0_domain}/"
        self.auth0_jwks_url = f"https://{self.auth0_domain}/.well-known/jwks.json"
        self.jwt_algorithms = ["RS256"]

settings = Settings()
```

This simple configuration class calculates the issuer URL and JWKS URL that will be used during token validation, and it sets the expected JWT algorithms to RS256, matching the API’s signing algorithm setting.[9][13][9][9][23][13]  

### Implementing JWT Validation with Auth0 JWKS

The core of the FastAPI integration is the ability to validate Auth0-issued JWT access tokens. Auth0 publishes its public keys at a standard JWKS endpoint under your tenant’s domain, allowing the API to verify the RS256 signatures associated with tokens without sharing private keys.[9][9][9][23][32] The JWKS is a JSON document containing an array of JWK objects, each representing a public key identified by a key ID (`kid`). When a client sends a Bearer token to the FastAPI API, the API must extract the token, decode the header to determine which key ID was used to sign it, look up the corresponding JWK in the JWKS, construct a suitable public key object, and then call `jwt.decode` with the token, key, expected algorithms, audience, and issuer.[23][32]  

A straightforward approach is to retrieve the JWKS once at application startup and cache it in memory, refreshing it manually if keys change. For illustrative purposes, the following code places the JWKS retrieval and token validation logic in a module such as `auth.py`:

```python
import json
from typing import Any, Dict, List

import requests
from fastapi import Depends, Header, HTTPException, status
from jose import jwt
from jose.utils import base64url_decode

from .config import settings

class Auth0JWKS:
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        response = requests.get(settings.auth0_jwks_url, timeout=5)
        response.raise_for_status()
        jwks_data = response.json()
        self.keys: List[Dict[str, Any]] = jwks_data.get("keys", [])

    def get_key(self, kid: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
        for key in self.keys:
            if key.get("kid") == kid:
                return key
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Unable to find appropriate key",
        )

jwks_cache = Auth0JWKS()

def get_token_auth_header(authorization: str = Header(..., alias="Authorization")) -> str:
    parts = authorization.split()
    if len(parts) != 2 or parts[0].lower() != "bearer":
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Authorization header must be Bearer token",
        )
    return parts[1]

def decode_token(token: str = Depends(get_token_auth_header)) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    try:
        unverified_header = jwt.get_unverified_header(token)
    except jwt.JWTError:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Invalid token header",
        )

    kid = unverified_header.get("kid")
    if not kid:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Token header missing kid",
        )

    jwk_data = jwks_cache.get_key(kid)
    public_key = jwt.algorithms.RSAAlgorithm.from_jwk(json.dumps(jwk_data))

    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(
            token,
            key=public_key,
            algorithms=settings.jwt_algorithms,
            audience=settings.auth0_audience,
            issuer=settings.auth0_issuer,
        )
    except jwt.ExpiredSignatureError:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Token has expired",
        )
    except jwt.JWTClaimsError:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Incorrect claims, please check audience and issuer",
        )
    except Exception:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Unable to parse authentication token",
        )

    return payload
```

This implementation follows the typical Auth0 pattern for validating RS256 JWTs: parse the unverified header, select the correct JWK from the JWKS based on the `kid`, construct the public key, and then call `jwt.decode` with the expected audience and issuer.[23][32] The `jwt.decode` function both decodes and verifies the token by default, confirming community guidance that decode and verification are integrated operations in libraries like `python-jose` and PyJWT.[10][23][32] The function raises FastAPI `HTTPException`s with appropriate HTTP status codes and messages when errors occur, allowing FastAPI’s exception handlers to generate clear HTTP responses.  

The `decode_token` function is declared as a dependency using FastAPI’s dependency injection. When used as a dependency in a path operation, it will automatically extract the Bearer token from the Authorization header, validate it, and return the decoded payload as a Python dictionary if successful; otherwise, it will short-circuit the request with an error response.[41]  

### Modeling the Current User and Permissions

To make authorization decisions more expressive and to enable richer type hints, you can wrap the decoded token payload into a Pydantic model that represents the current user and their permissions. FastAPI’s security tutorial demonstrates how to use Pydantic models to encapsulate user data returned by a token decoding function so that they can be injected into endpoints as well-typed objects.[41] In the context of Auth0 RBAC, the most relevant fields from the token payload are the user’s subject identifier (`sub`) and the `permissions` array claim that Auth0 includes when RBAC is enabled and configured to add permissions to the access token.[2][5][2][13][2][19][24][13]  

You can add the following to a module such as `models.py`:

```python
from typing import List, Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel

class CurrentUser(BaseModel):
    sub: str
    permissions: List[str] = []
    scope: Optional[str] = None
```

Then you create a dependency function `get_current_user` that depends on `decode_token` and populates a `CurrentUser` object:

```python
from typing import Dict, Any

from fastapi import Depends

from .auth import decode_token
from .models import CurrentUser

def get_current_user(payload: Dict[str, Any] = Depends(decode_token)) -> CurrentUser:
    sub = payload.get("sub")
    permissions = payload.get("permissions", [])
    scope = payload.get("scope")
    return CurrentUser(sub=sub, permissions=permissions, scope=scope)
```

This approach encapsulates the raw JWT payload in a structured object, making it easy to inject the current user into path operation functions and to inspect permissions using Python’s list operations.[27][41]  

### Implementing Permission-Based RBAC Dependencies

With the `CurrentUser` model in place, you can implement a reusable dependency that enforces permission-based RBAC by checking whether the current user has a required permission in their `permissions` array. The App Generator guide on RBAC in FastAPI demonstrates how to create a dependency class that checks permissions from a JWT’s payload and raises HTTP 403 Forbidden if the user lacks the required permission, which aligns perfectly with Auth0’s permission-based RBAC model.[27]  

You can implement a simple permission checker as follows in a module such as `rbac.py`:

```python
from typing import Callable, List, Optional

from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException, status

from .models import CurrentUser
from .auth import decode_token
from .models import CurrentUser

def require_permission(required_permission: str) -> Callable[[CurrentUser], None]:
    def checker(user: CurrentUser = Depends(get_current_user)) -> None:
        if required_permission not in user.permissions:
            raise HTTPException(
                status_code=status.HTTP_403_FORBIDDEN,
                detail=f"Permission '{required_permission}' is required",
            )
    return checker
```

This factory function returns a dependency function that, when used in a path operation, will automatically retrieve the current user via token validation and then examine the `permissions` list to ensure that `required_permission` is present, raising an exception if not.[27] For situations where an endpoint requires multiple permissions, you can extend this pattern to accept a list of required permissions and check that all or any of them are present, according to your policy.  

The design recognizes a critical security principle: the backend does not trust client-side assertions about permissions or roles; instead, it trusts Auth0’s signed token as the authoritative representation of the user’s privileges and uses that token to enforce authorization decisions. This approach ensures that even if a malicious client tampers with JavaScript or local storage, the backend will still verify the token’s signature and claims before authorizing requests.[5][23][27][32]  

### Creating the FastAPI Application and Routes

With configuration, JWT validation, user modeling, and RBAC dependencies established, you can now wire them into a FastAPI application with public, authenticated, and permission-protected endpoints. The `app.py` file at the root of your project might look as follows:

```python
from typing import Any, Dict, List

from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

from .config import settings
from .models import CurrentUser
from .auth import decode_token
from .rbac import require_permission, get_current_user

app = FastAPI(title="Auth0 RBAC FastAPI API")

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["http://localhost:4040", "http://localhost:3000"],
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)

@app.get("/api/public")
def public_endpoint() -> Dict[str, str]:
    return {"message": "This is a public endpoint; no authentication required."}

@app.get("/api/private")
def private_endpoint(user: CurrentUser = Depends(get_current_user)) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    return {
        "message": "This is a private endpoint, authenticated users only.",
        "user": {"sub": user.sub, "permissions": user.permissions},
    }

@app.get("/api/messages")
def read_messages(
    user: CurrentUser = Depends(get_current_user),
    _: None = Depends(require_permission("read:messages")),
) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    messages: List[str] = ["Message 1", "Message 2"]
    return {
        "message": "You have permission to read messages.",
        "items": messages,
    }

@app.post("/api/messages")
def create_message(
    user: CurrentUser = Depends(get_current_user),
    _: None = Depends(require_permission("write:messages")),
) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    return {
        "message": "You have permission to create messages.",
        "created_by": user.sub,
    }
```

The `CORSMiddleware` configuration allows the React SPA’s origin to call the API and supports credentials and all HTTP methods and headers, which is typical when a single SPA interacts with a single API under development.[9][13][9][9][26][13] In production, you should narrow `allow_origins` to known origins corresponding to your deployed client applications. The `/api/public` endpoint is accessible without authentication, demonstrating an open route. The `/api/private` endpoint requires authentication but no specific permission; it uses `get_current_user` to enforce that a valid token is present and to return basic information about the user. The `/api/messages` GET and POST endpoints apply the `require_permission` dependency with `read:messages` and `write:messages` respectively, enforcing RBAC based on Auth0’s permissions claim.  

When the React SPA calls `/api/messages` with an access token that lacks `read:messages` in the `permissions` array, `require_permission` will raise an HTTP 403 Forbidden error. Conversely, an access token for a user with the appropriate role and permission will be accepted, and the endpoint will return the data or perform the requested side effect. This pattern makes authorization logic explicit and centralized, and it builds directly on Auth0’s RBAC configuration.[5][13][18][27][13]  

### Running and Testing the FastAPI API Manually

To run the API locally, you activate your virtual environment and start Uvicorn:

```bash
source venv/bin/activate
uvicorn app:app --reload
```

By default, the API will listen on `http://127.0.0.1:8000`, with the defined endpoints reachable at paths such as `/api/public` and `/api/private`. You can verify the public endpoint without authentication by fetching it in a browser or with curl. For the protected endpoints, you need a valid Auth0 access token.[9][13][9][9][13]  

During development, Auth0’s dashboard provides a convenient way to obtain a test access token for your API. Within the APIs section, you select your FastAPI API, go to the Test tab, and use the “Asking Auth0 for tokens from my application” section where Auth0 generates a sample client credentials cURL request and a ready-made access token that you can copy.[9][13][9][9][13] Although client credentials flow is more appropriate for machine-to-machine use and does not represent an end-user, this token is still a valid JWT for your API and can be used to exercise your backend’s token validation and permission enforcement logic. For user-centered flows, you will eventually obtain access tokens from the React SPA via the Auth0 React SDK, as described later.  

With an access token in hand, you can call the protected endpoint using curl:

```bash
curl -X GET \
  http://localhost:8000/api/private \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN"
```

If the token is valid and has the correct audience and issuer, the API will respond with the message and user data. To test RBAC, you can assign permissions to the machine-to-machine client or, more realistically, use a token obtained by a real user login from the React SPA, as described in the next sections.[9][13][9][19][9][24][13]  

## Implementing the React SPA with Auth0 New Universal Login and RBAC

### React Application Setup and Auth0 React SDK Installation

On the frontend, the React SPA is responsible for initiating login with Auth0, managing the user session through the Auth0 React SDK, and calling the FastAPI API with access tokens. Auth0 provides an official React SDK, `@auth0/auth0-react`, that implements the Authorization Code Flow with PKCE under the hood, manages token expiration and renewal, and integrates with React Context to expose authentication state, methods, and user information via a hook.[29][34]  

Assuming you have an existing React application created with Create React App, Vite, or other tooling, you navigate to the project directory and install the Auth0 React SDK using npm or yarn:

```bash
npm install @auth0/auth0-react
```

or

```bash
yarn add @auth0/auth0-react
```

The SDK is published and maintained by Auth0 and has a semantic versioning scheme; for example, version 2.15.1 corresponds to a recent release that supports React hooks and modern React Router patterns.[29][39] The SDK’s documentation confirms that it uses React Context under the hood, exposing the `Auth0Provider` component that wraps your application and a `useAuth0` hook that exposes properties such as `isAuthenticated`, `user`, `loginWithRedirect`, `logout`, `getAccessTokenSilently`, and others.[6][6][29][34]  

Just as with the FastAPI backend, you should configure the SPA using environment variables so that tokens are requested for the correct Auth0 domain and API audience. In the React project’s root, you can create an `.env` file containing:

```ini
REACT_APP_AUTH0_DOMAIN=your-tenant.us.auth0.com
REACT_APP_AUTH0_CLIENT_ID=your_spa_client_id
REACT_APP_AUTH0_AUDIENCE=https://my-fastapi-api
REACT_APP_API_SERVER_URL=http://localhost:8000
```

Auth0’s React guides emphasize that the Auth0 Domain and Client ID must correspond to the SPA application’s settings page, and that the audience must match the API Identifier you configured for the FastAPI backend; passing the audience ensures that access tokens issued via `getAccessTokenSilently` are JWTs with the correct `aud` claim and not opaque tokens that are only valid for the default resource server.[6][15][6][26][29] If you previously compiled your app without specifying an audience and later add it, you may obtain opaque tokens until you restart the development server due to caching; community discussions highlight restarting the app or process as a remedy.[15]  

### Wrapping the React App with Auth0Provider
